one_line_takeaway: Randomly zeroing a Bernoulli-selected subset of shared model parameters defends federated learning against gradient-leakage image reconstruction (APRIL and adversarial-optimization attacks) while keeping accuracy near standard FL, outperforming differential privacy's privacy-accuracy tradeoff.
key_finding: FLRSP breaks the privacy-vs-accuracy tradeoff of differential privacy: under the adversarial-optimization attack on ResNet, standard FL leaks images (SSIM ~0.75-0.87 at Acc 0.960) and DP either fails to protect or wrecks accuracy (eps=1: Acc 0.492 with some SSIM up to 0.677/0.772; eps=4: Acc 0.926 with SSIM up to 0.826/0.844; 6 of 15 images almost fully restored for every eps), whereas FLRSP drives SSIM down to as low as 0.005-0.237 while holding accuracy at ~0.95 (R=0.2 Acc 0.9568; R=0.5 Acc 0.9525; R=0.8 Acc 0.9536). Against APRIL on ViT it matches the fixed-position defense even at R=0.2 while, unlike fixed-position, preserving accuracy when training from scratch.
core_contribution: The paper proposes FLRSP (Federated Learning using Randomly Selected model Parameters), a defense for privacy-preserving federated learning against image-reconstruction (gradient-leakage) attacks. Each client, after local training, applies a per-parameter binary random mask B ~ Bernoulli(R) (R = probability of zeroing a parameter) to its computed gradients/weights, so only a randomly selected subset of parameters is shared with the server; the server aggregates only the non-zero contributions (dividing by the count of non-zero clients per parameter). Zeroing gradient terms breaks the equations the attacks rely on: it nulls terms of the APRIL closed-form restoration (Eqs.1-2) for ViT and disrupts the gradient cosine-similarity objective (Eq.5) used by adversarial-optimization attacks on CNNs. The authors explicitly frame this as EXPANDING and generalizing an earlier concept from cited ref [32] (random binary weights, limited to ViT + FedSGD): their own additions are (i) support for FedAvg in addition to FedSGD, (ii) ResNet34 (a CNN) in addition to ViT, (iii) comparison against differential privacy, and (iv) evaluation against adversarial-optimization reconstruction. They also give an informal convergence argument (FLRSP is equivalent to standard FL with a scaled learning rate).
threat_model: An adversary (curious/malicious central server, another client, or an external third party) observes the model updates that clients share each round (gradients under FedSGD, weights/biases under FedAvg) and attempts to reconstruct clients' private training images. The attacker is assumed to know the shared updates AND the security-enhancement algorithm, including the selection probability R. Two concrete reconstruction attacks are considered: APRIL, a closed-form attention-privacy-leakage attack specific to ViT, and an adversarial-optimization attack (gradient cosine-similarity minimization) effective against CNNs. Related membership-inference and property-inference attacks are mentioned but not the evaluation target. FLRSP's defense: clients pre-share a random binary mask that zeros a fraction R of parameters, so the algebraic conditions the attacks exploit no longer hold.
limitations: The choice of R is not principled: it is task-, client-count-, non-i.i.d.-degree-, and attack-dependent, and must be selected experimentally in advance (suggested criterion: keep reconstruction SSIM below 0.5). Under FedAvg, FLRSP shows low accuracy in the early training phase (severe at R=0.8) because per-epoch parameter magnitudes are larger than per-batch gradients; it only converges to standard-FL accuracy with continued training and an appropriate R. Attack evaluation is small-scale (only 15 images, SSIM-based, single dataset CIFAR-10). Non-i.i.d. data (Dirichlet a=0.1) degrades FLRSP accuracy (though still above DP). The convergence 'analysis' is informal - it merely rewrites the standard bound with a rescaled learning rate rather than proving a new rate. Attack robustness is chiefly argued for FedSGD, with FedAvg resistance asserted by analogy rather than fully measured.

datasets_used: ImageNet (used for pretraining ViT and ResNet34)
Non-i.i.d. split via Dirichlet distribution (hyperparameter a=0.1)
quantitative_results: Standard FL adversarial-optimization reconstruction on ResNet: SSIM 0.789/0.746/0.868/0.830, Acc=0.960
DP: 6 of 15 images almost completely restored for every eps value tested
FLRSP achieved lower SSIM than both DP and standard FL for all R values (R=0.2,0.5,0.8)
FLRSP robust against APRIL on ViT even at R=0.2, matching fixed-position defense
N=5 clients, 10,000 images each; ViT LR 1e-5, ResNet LR 1e-4, batch size 32; DP delta=0.5, eps in {1,2,4}
Adversarial-optimization attack: Adam, LR 1e-4, 24,000 iterations, batch size 1, 15 images
Reported gradient/parameter magnitude ranges: FedSGD gradients 0.01-0.0001 vs FedAvg parameters 10-0.1
baselines_compared: Fixed-position method [25] (ViT-specific defense zeroing positional-embedding updates)
